title: Target Operating Model (TOM)
lead: The Target Operating Model is the designed future state of an organisation's IT function — the blueprint that defines where you are going and what the organisation will look like when you get there.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: The Target Operating Model (TOM) is the concrete answer to the question: 'What should our IT function look like in the future?' It is the centrepiece deliverable of every ITOM engagement. While the current operating model describes how things work today, the TOM defines the desired future state — across people, processes, technology, governance, and sourcing. The TOM is not a vision statement. It is an actionable blueprint precise enough to drive transformation planning, resource allocation, and implementation decisions.

items.desc: how the IT function operates today — documented through assessment, interviews, and process analysis
the difference between current and target across all operating model dimensions — identifies what must change
the TOM — the desired future design that closes the gaps and realises the strategic ambition
the sequenced plan for moving from AS-IS to TOM — waves, dependencies, investment, and change management
target team structure, roles, capabilities, spans of control, and ways of working in the future state
redesigned end-to-end processes — from strategic planning through operational delivery and continual improvement
who decides what in the future model — governance bodies, policies, accountabilities, and escalation paths
the target application landscape, platforms, and toolchain that enable the new operating model
what is done in-house, what is outsourced, and how providers are managed in the target state
target KPIs, SLAs, and management information that demonstrate the TOM is delivering its intended value
explicitly linked to business strategy, digital ambition, and IT direction — not designed in isolation
concrete enough to drive decisions — not a high-level picture but a usable design document
developed with the people who will live and work in it — increases quality and adoption
designed with the transformation journey in mind — what can be achieved in waves, what is a longer-term ambition
includes clear success criteria so progress toward the TOM can be tracked and demonstrated
TOM design — structured design of the target operating model across all dimensions, from assessment to blueprint
Operating Model Canvas workshops — co-create the TOM with business and IT stakeholders using the (IT)OM Canvas
Gap analysis — compare current and target operating model to identify and prioritise transformation requirements
Transformation roadmap — translate the TOM into a phased implementation plan with clear milestones and investment cases
TOM validation — independent review of a draft TOM to stress-test completeness, coherence, and strategic alignment
